coredata.dc:title: GQDs/hMSN nanoplatform: Singlet oxygen generation for photodynamic therapy
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Jingjing
Zhang, Yan
Guo, Pengyue
Xu, Huanghuang
Wang, Yuan
Yang, Dongzhi
coredata.dc:description: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has received extensive attention in tumor treatment due to its advantages in overcoming drug resistance for chemotherapy. In this work, we prepared a nanoplatform generated from graphene quantum dots capped in hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (GQDs/hMSN). The singlet oxygen (1O2) generation efficiency and photodynamic therapy effect of GQDs/hMSN were studied in vitro and in vivo. GQDs/hMSN had good 1O2 generation yield in water. Based on the findings from cellular experiments, we concluded that doxorubicin (DOX) loaded GQDs/hMSN (GQDs/hMSN(DOX)) had synergistic chemo-photodynamic therapeutic effect. From in vivo therapy on HeLa tumor-bearing mice, GQDs/hMSN exhibited significant accumulation in the tumor site, where DOX was also released from the nanoplatform due to the unique tumor microenvironment. This work further confirmed the usefulness of GQDs/hMSN nanoplatform as a potentially useful vector for combinational therapy of cancer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs
GQDs/hMSN
Singlet oxygen
Photodynamic therapy
Cervical cancer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The combined killing effect of GQDs/hMSN(DOX)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural and morphological characteristics of the prepared GQDs, hMSN, and GQDs/hMSN. (A) TEM image of GQDs. (B) TEM image of hMSN. (C) TEM image of GQDs/hMSN. (D) DLS of GQDs, hMSN, and GQDs/hMSN.

Optical properties of GQDs and GQDs/hMSN conjugates. (A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs and GQDs/hMSN conjugates. (B) Excitation spectra of GQDs and GQDs/hMSN conjugates. (C) Emission spectra of GQDs and GQDs/hMSN conjugates. (D) DOX release profile from GQDs/hMSN(DOX)-PEG in different media.

Singlet oxygen assay of GQDs/hMSN conjugates. (A) The absorbance curve of Na2-ADPA trigged by GQDs with laser irradiation time. (B) The absorbance curve of Na2-ADPA trigged by GQDs/hMSN with laser irradiation time. (C) 1O2 generation in HeLa cells with DCFH-DA as probe, Rosup as the positive control (Scale bar: 100 μm). (D) Fluorescence intensity calculated from cell imaging (Laser irradiation: 635 nm, 20 mW/cm2). Cellular uptake and 1O2 generation in vitro.

The combined therapy of GQDs and GQDs/hMSN conjugates in vitro. (A) HeLa cells viabilities treated by GQDs or GQDs/hMSN. (B) HeLa cells viabilities treated by GQDs/hMSN-PEG or GQDs/hMSN(DOX)-PEG without or with laser irradiation (635 nm, 20 mW/cm2, 2 min).

In vivo anticancer therapy of GQDs/hMSN-PEG and GQDs/hMSN(DOX)-PEG. (A) The actual tumor volume of four groups of Balb/c mice on day 0, day 5, and day 10 post-injection of saline, GQDs/hMSN-PEG, GQDs/hMSN-PEG + Laser and GQDs/hMSN(DOX)-PEG + Laser. Tumor with different treatments were collected on 10 days were listed on the right row. (B) The curve of tumor inhibition treated by GQDs/hMSN-PEG, GQDs/hMSN-PEG + Laser and GQDs/hMSN(DOX)-PEG + Laser, respectively (Saline was the control group). (C) H&E stained tumor slides collected from tumor-bearing mice on day 10 (n = 3). Scale bar: 100 μm.
The distribution of GQDs/hMSN in the tumor by observing tumor frozen section using fluorescence microscope.

head.ce:title: GQDs/hMSN nanoplatform: Singlet oxygen generation for photodynamic therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of New Drug Research and Clinical Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, Jiangsu, 221004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of New Drug Research and Clinical Pharmacy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of New Drug Research and Clinical Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, Jiangsu, 221004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hospital 904 of China Joint Logistic Support Force, Changzhou, Jiangsu, 213000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hospital 904 of China Joint Logistic Support Force

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hospital 904 of China Joint Logistic Support Force, Changzhou, Jiangsu, 213000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, Affiliated Wuxi Children's Hospital, Nanjing Medical University, Wuxi, Jiangsu, 214025, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacy, Affiliated Wuxi Children's Hospital, Nanjing Medical University, Wuxi, Jiangsu, 214025, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has received extensive attention in tumor treatment due to its advantages in overcoming drug resistance for chemotherapy. In this work, we prepared a nanoplatform generated from graphene quantum dots capped in hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (GQDs/hMSN). The singlet oxygen (1O2) generation efficiency and photodynamic therapy effect of GQDs/hMSN were studied in vitro and in vivo. GQDs/hMSN had good 1O2 generation yield in water. Based on the findings from cellular experiments, we concluded that doxorubicin (DOX) loaded GQDs/hMSN (GQDs/hMSN(DOX)) had synergistic chemo-photodynamic therapeutic effect. From in vivo therapy on HeLa tumor-bearing mice, GQDs/hMSN exhibited significant accumulation in the tumor site, where DOX was also released from the nanoplatform due to the unique tumor microenvironment. This work further confirmed the usefulness of GQDs/hMSN nanoplatform as a potentially useful vector for combinational therapy of cancer.

ce:section.ce:para: Although early detection, effective screening and selective intervention can reduce the mortality of cervical cancer patients [1–3], it is still one of the most common gynecological malignancies, ranking fourth among all female cancer types [4,5]. In addition to traditional surgery, radiotherapy and chemotherapy, a variety of new cancer treatment methods have emerged. Among them, photodynamic therapy (PDT) has attracted widespread attention due to its minimally invasive characteristic [6–10]. Three essential factors are required in PDT: laser, photosensitizer (PS) and oxygen [11,12], where PS can produce singlet oxygen (1O2) to kill cancer cells [13]. It is extremely difficult for most traditional photosensitizers to go to the clinical stage due to their poor water solubility, low yield of 1O2 and short life of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [14,15]. Therefore, it is of great significance for us to develop new efficient photosensitizers with improvement in these properties.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), i.e. graphite fragments with a diameter of less than 10 nm, have good biocompatibility and strong fluorescence [16,17], and some QDs have photodynamic properties. Ge and coworkers [18] reported that GQDs synthesized from polythiophene have good two-photon absorption efficiency, with highly stable two-photon excitation (TPE) located in the near infrared region. Under specific laser irradiation, the singlet oxygen (1O2) yield of GQDs can reach 1.3, which makes it useful in bioimaging and photodynamic therapy [19,20]. The size of nanomaterials directly affects their in vivo pharmacokinetics. For example, GQDs with a size of 2–6 nm can be easily excreted via renal pathways before reaching the lesion site [21,22]. Therefore, further GQDs modification before in vivo application is particularly important, and coating by inorganic materials is one useful and straightforward strategy. Among all the available inorganic materials, hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (hMSNs) are frequently used in various biomedical applications due to their good biocompatibility and cargo loading properties [23–25]. To date, it has been used in accommodating different cargos, including hydrophobic anticancer drugs, proteins and therapeutic genes.

In this work, photoresponsive GQDs were used as the photosensitizer, hMSN was adopted as the delivery nanoshell and doxorubicin (DOX) was chosen as the model drug. The constructed GQDs/hMSN(DOX) has a synergistic chemotherapy-PDT efficacy. The optical properties and structural characteristics, as well as the safety and PDT efficiency of GQDs/hMSN(DOX) were systematically studied. 1O2 generation efficiency and killing effect on cervical cancer cells were verified in a series of cellular experiments. The accumulation and therapeutic effect of prepared GQDs/hMSN(DOX) was also studied in HeLa tumor-bearing mice.

Thiophene-3-boronic acid, 4-bromobenzyl bromide, Pd(PPh3)4, and N, N-dimethyldodecylamine were purchased from Alfa Aesar (China). SCM-PEG5k-Mal (i.e. succinimidyl carboxymethyl PEG maleimide, MW: 5 kDa) were purchased from YareBio (Shanghai, China). 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (APS) and hexadecyl trimethylammonium chloride (CTAC) were acquired from J&K Scientific Ltd (Beijing, China). Doxorubicin (DOX), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and triethylamine (TEA), 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) and Bengal rose (RB) were all purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Anthracene-9,10-dipropionic acid disodium salt (ADPA-Na2) was purchased from Abcom (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco's modification of Eagle's medium Dulbecco (DMEM) cells culture medium, fetal bovine serum (FBS), trypsin, 2-(4-Amidinophenyl)-6-indolecarbamidine dihydrochloride (DAPI), 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) and 1O2 test kits were purchased from Vicmed (Xuzhou, China). All the other reagents were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical reagent (Beijing, China). All chemical reagents were directly used without further purification following manufacturer's instructions. All buffers were prepared from Millipore-grade water.

HeLa and L929 cells were purchased from Shanghai Institutes for Biological Sciences (Shanghai, China). Six-week-old female nude mice were purchased from Model Animal Research Center of Nanjing University (Nanjing, China).

GQDs/hMSN nanoparticles were prepared according to our previous work with minor modifications [26,27]. First, all the reagents was deoxygenated with nitrogen. 4-bromobenzyl bromide and N,N-dimethyldodecylamine were reacted in a mixed solution of CH2Cl2/CH3OH (V:V = 3:2) for 12 h, to acquire compound 1. By using Pd(PPh3)4 as the catalyst, compound 1 was reacted with thiophene-3-boronic acid in alkaline solution to obtain compound 2. Subsequently, compound 2 was polymerized to form PT2, which underwent a hydrothermal reaction at 160 °C for 24 h to prepare GQDs. Excessive reagents were removed through a 0.22 μm filter. With the prepared GQDs as the crystalization seeds, dense silicon dioxide (dSiO2) was formed through the hydrolysis of TEOS under the alkaline environment to produce GQDs/dSiO2 [28]. After washing with water and ethanol, TEOS was added drop by drop into GQDs/dSiO2 solution and allowed its reaction with CTAC as the soft template at 80 °C for 1.5 h, to synthesize GQDs/dSiO2/SiO2. After being etched by Na2CO3 for 45 min at 50 °C, and washed with 1% NaCl methanol mixture, the GQDs/hMSN was acquired. Amino groups were modified on the surface of GQDs/hMSN by the hydrolysis of APS to get GQDs/hMSN-NH2. Further surface modification by PEG (the product was GQDs/hMSN-PEG) was achieved by the reaction between SCM-PEG-Mal and amino group.

The morphology and size of GQDs, hMSN, and GQDs/hMSN conjugates were measured via a Tecnai G2 Spirit Twin transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI, USA), and dynamic light scattering (DLS) (380 ZLS, Nicomp, USA). The optical properties of these nanoconjugates were recorded by UV-6100 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (MAPADA, Shanghai), and F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). Electron-spin resonance (ESR) measurements were carried out by using an ESR spectrometer (A300, Bruker).

DOX was loaded into GQDs/hMSN conjugates by mixing (pH 8.0) and stirring for 24 h, the unloaded DOX was removed by a 10 KD centrifugation filter. Acquired GQDs/hMSN(DOX) conjugates were washed with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) for three times. The drug loading and release capacity of GQDs/hMSN conjugates was evaluated by measuring the absorbance of DOX, where the concentration of DOX could be determined by analyzing its absorbance at 490 nm. DOX release behavior of GQD/hMSN(DOX) in different media was also determined.

GQDs conjugates were respectively dispersed in water, PBS, fetal bovine serum (FBS) or DMEM medium and subsequently mixed with Na2-ADPA. The singlet oxygen yields from GQDs and GQDs@hMSN conjugates was determined by using water-soluble Na2-ADPA as the 1O2-trapping agent. RB was used as the control reagent. Under the irradiation at 635 nm, the absorbance of Na2-ADPA at 378 nm was recorded at different irradiation time [29,30]. 1O2 yield was calculated according to the following equation:

Here, was the 1O2 yield of sample, ϕ RB was the 1O2 yield of RB (0.75 in water). K S and K RB, were the decomposition rate constants of Na2-ADPA affected by GQDs conjugates and RB, respectively. A S and A RB was the absorbance of GQDs conjugates and RB which were determined by integration of the optical absorption bands in the wavelength range 400–700 nm, respectively.

Adherent HeLa cells were incubated with GQDs/hMSN for 0.5 h, 1 h, 1.5 h and 2 h, respectively, followed by repeated washing with PBS and fixing with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10 min. The cells were then stained with DAPI for additional 10 min, and observed through a fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Japan) to study the cellular uptake behavior of GQDs/hMSN.

DCFH-DA was used as the fluorescent probe to measure the intracellular 1O2 generation. DCFH-DA was firstly incubated with HeLa cells for 20 min. Subsequently, GQDs/hMSN at different concentrations were added into cells medium and incubated for additional 2 h. After that, the cells were irradiated with a 635 nm laser (20 mW/cm2) for 2 min. After incubating for another 0.5 h, the fluorescence images were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 488 nm using a fluorescence microscope. Rosup was used as the control reagent.

The in vitro combined therapeutic efficacy of GQDs/hMSN(DOX) was evaluated through detecting HeLa cells viability after incubating with GQDs or GQDs/hMSN conjugates. The incubation time was 2 h. MTT assay and live/dead cell staining were carried out to study the photodynamic effect by comparing the cells viability treated with or without 635 nm irradiation (20 mW/cm2, 2 min).

All animal experiments complied with the ARRIVE guidelines and were carried out in accordance with the National Institutes of Health Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of USA (NIH Publications No. 8023, revised 1978) and approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Xuzhou Medical University (License No. SYXK2010-0011). Female nude mice (6 weeks, 18–22 g) were subcutaneously injected with 1 × 106 of HeLa cells to establish the tumor models. When the tumor diameter reaches 5–8 mm, in vivo studies were carried out. HeLa tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into four groups (n = 6 per group). They were intravenously injected with saline (control group), GQDs/hMSN-PEG (100 μg/kg based on GQDs), GQDs/hMSN-PEG (100 μg/kg based on GQDs) and GQDs/hMSN(DOX)-PEG (100 μg/kg based on GQDs), respectively, followed by laser treatment (635 nm, 300 mW/cm2 for 10 min) for the last two group. The size of the tumors was measured every day for 10 days. After treatment, HeLa tumor-bearing mice were sacrificed, and the tumors were collected for histology examinations. The tissues were frozen and sectioned into 7 μm thick slices, then stained with DAPI as well as hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) respectively. The tissue slices were examined by an IX73 digital microscope (Olympus, Japan) with a magnitude of 200 × .

The morphology and size of GQDs, hMSN and GQDs/hMSN were evaluated by TEM and DLS. As shown in Fig. 1 , the diameter of GQDs, hMSN and GQDs/hMSN was about 5 nm, 130 nm and 135 nm, respectively. hMSN exhibited a hollow and mesoporous structure, and GQDs were located inside hMSN. The particle size (16, 190 and 205 nm for GQDs, hMSN and GQDs/hMSN, respectively) measured by DLS was larger than that measured by TEM might result from the surface hydration of these nanoconjugates.

GQDs and DOX has the specialized absorption at 450 nm and 490 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the strong absorbance of GQDs/hMSN conjugates at about 450 nm, together with TEM results, indicated the successful loading of GQDs into hMSN. The appearance of the specialized absorption at about 490 nm from GQDs/hMSN(DOX)-PEG served as the strong evidence for the successful loading of DOX. As shown in Fig. 2C, the maximum emission wavelength of GQDs/hMSN conjugates showed about 35 nm blue-shift (from 625 nm to 590 nm) due to quantum size effect. If we fixed the emission wavelength, the same excitation spectra of GQDs and GQDs/hMSN conjugates were acquired, which further proved the formation of GQDs/hMSN conjugates.

The DOX loading and release was evaluated by UV–vis absorption, where the absorbance at 490 nm was in direct proportion to the concentration of DOX. Results showed that the loading capacity of DOX in GQDs/hMSN conjugates was 0.763 mg DOX/mg GQDs/hMSN-PEG. The releasing profiles of DOX from the nanoconjugates at the pH of 5.0, 6.5 and 7.4 was also tested. As shown in Fig. 2D, the release behavior of DOX from GQDs/hMSN-PEG was obviously pH-dependent. The release amounts of DOX from GQDs/hMSN-PEG at pH 5.0, 6.5 and 7.4 were 61.15%, 35.66% and 15.28% (0.467, 0.272, 0.117 mg DOX/mg GQDs/hMSN-PEG), respectively, at the time of 100 h post-incubation.

GQDs has the ability of 1O2 generation, which can be greatly enhanced under laser irradiation. Firstly, ESR technique was employed to confirm the 1O2 generation by the GQDs/hMSN under laser irradiation. TEMP was selected as the spin trap for 1O2. As illustrated in Fig. S1, a strong 1O2-induced signal, 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl, was observed in the ESR spectra rapidly after laser irradiation. In contrast, no significant ESR signal was observed if the GQDs/hMSN were kept in the dark. The 1O2 generation ability of GQDs and GQDs/hMSN was also measured by a chemical method. Na2-ADPA was used as the probe, in which the absorbance of Na2-ADPA (378 nm) was inversely proportional to the amount of 1O2. With RB being used as the control ( = 0.75), the 1O2 generation efficiency of GQDs and GQDs/hMSN dispersed in different solution including water, PBS, FBS, or DMEM was measured after the laser irradiation (635 nm, 20 mW/cm2). Results indicated that in water was 0.66, while that of GQDs/hMSN was 0.47. Various ions in PBS, FBS and DMEM medium could affect the 1O2 generation ability, resulting in lower 1O2 generation efficiency.

For evaluating the biological behavior of GQDs/hMSN conjugates, the cell uptake was carried out by detecting the GQDs fluorescence intensity after incubating with HeLa cells, where the green fluorescence from GQDs indicated the location of GQDs/hMSN. As shown in Figs. S2 and S3, no significant fluorescence signal of GQDs was observed at 0.5 h, and the signal gradually increased from 1 h to 1.5 h, and there was no significant change with the extension of time to 2 h. Thus, in the following in vitro analysis, the incubation time of 2 h was selected.

DCFH-DA was easily taken up by cells and hydrolyzed. Its hydrolyzed product could be oxidized by 1O2 to produce fluorescent substances, so it was suitable for the detection of 1O2 in cells. The GQDs/hMSN conjugates was incubated with HeLa cells for 2 h, and then the system was irradiated at 635 nm (20 mW/cm2) for 2 min. Finally the in vitro generation of 1O2 was evaluated by measuring the green fluorescence intensity (Ex = 488 nm). From Fig. 3 C–D, the 1O2 generation efficiency of GQDs/hMSN conjugates was significantly higher than that of positive control, Rosup (p < 0.05). The PDT effect of GQDs/hMSN was also confirmed by live/dead cell staining. As illustrated in Fig. S4, with the assistance of irradiation, GQDs and GQDs/hMSN showed the significant cell killing effect. In comparison, no obvious dead cells were observed in GQDs group (without irradiation).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The combined killing effect of GQDs/hMSN(DOX)
ce:section.ce:para: The cells safety of GQDs/hMSN conjugates was evaluated by an MTT assay on tumor cells (HeLa) and normal cells (L929). Results in Fig. S5 indicated that GQDs and GQDs/hMSN at different concentrations did not show significant cytotoxicity in both HeLa and L929 cells. GQDs endowed GQDs/hMSN(DOX) with photodynamic therapy capacity, and the low pH-triggered release of DOX gave the nanosystems chemotherapeutic abilities. Herein, the effect of GQDs, GQDs/hMSN, GQDs/hMSN-PEG and GQDs/hMSN(DOX)-PEG, as well as laser irradiation, on HeLa cell viability were testified by the MTT assay. The experimental conditions in control group (without laser) was the same as that of laser irradiation group. As shown in Fig. 4 A and B, without laser irradiation, all HeLa cells treated by GQDs, GQDs/hMSN and GQDs/hMSN-PEG showed a cell viability higher than 80%, which indicated that these three kinds of nanosystems were biologically safe for HeLa cells. The drug loading system, GQDs/hMSN(DOX)-PEG, exhibited the cells killing effect with the survival rate decreasing by 12.0–21.1% compared with that of GQDs/hMSN-PEG, suggesting the chemotherapeutic effect of DOX from nano-drug delivery system. In contrast, if the laser irradiation was applied, the cell viability of HeLa cells treated with GQDs, GQDs/hMSN, GQDs/hMSN-PEG and GQDs/hMSN(DOX)-PEG further decreased by 9.0–33.5% compared with that of the corresponding non-laser irradiation group. Compared with GQDs, the photodynamic effect of GQDs/hMSN showed no significant difference. Furthermore, it was not affected by the PEG modification.

HeLa cells were subcutaneously injected into nude mice to establish the tumor model. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining in Fig. 5 C showed the abnormally clustered tumor cells and irregular nuclei morphology, which confirmed the existence and location of HeLa tumor nodules. When the diameter of the tumors reached 5–8 mm, the mice were divided into four groups (n = 6) and treated by saline, GQDs/hMSN-PEG, GQDs/hMSN-PEG + laser and GQDs/hMSN(DOX)-PEG + laser, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5A, tumors in the control group (saline treated) grew rapidly. For the GQDs/hMSN-PEG group, the tumor growth was not significantly inhibited. However, the growth of tumors in groups of GQDs/hMSN-PEG + Laser and GQDs/hMSN(DOX)-PEG + Laser was significantly slowed. Specifically, the tumor burden from GQDs/hMSN(DOX)-PEG + Laser group was less than half of that in GQDs/hMSN-PEG + Laser group. This significant difference was shown in the tumor inhibition in Fig. 5B. In the early stage of treatment (<5 days), GQDs/hMSN(DOX)-PEG + Laser showed a better therapeutic effect than that of GQDs/hMSN-PEG + Laser, however the tumor size difference between the two groups became smaller after 5 days, which was consistent with the release behavior of DOX.

For the histology analysis, the red fluorescence in Fig. 6 was attributed to the presence of GQDs/hMSN conjugates in all the examined groups. From the intensity and distribution of red fluorescence indicated in Fig. 6, there were substantial amounts of GQDs/hMSN conjugates accumulated in HeLa tumor, which was attributed to the high permeability and retention effect of nanomaterials in solid tumors. The overlap of red and blue fluorescence confirmed that GQDs/hMSN conjugates could enter tumor cells, which was consistent with the results of cell uptake.

In this work, the synergistic photodynamic/chemotherapeutic effect of GQDs/hMSN conjugates was studied in vitro and in vivo. Under laser irradiation, GQDs/hMSN showed significant 1O2 generation efficiency from the in vitro assay. The cells killing experiments results indicated that GQDs/hMSN conjugates exhibited both the photodynamic and chemotherapeutic effect, where the cell survival rate of HeLa cells treated by GQDs, GQDs/hMSN-PEG, GQDs/hMSN-PEG and GQDs/hMSN(DOX)-PEG with laser irradiation was significantly lower than that of non-laser irradiation groups (p < 0.05). Animal experimental results indicated that GQDs/hMSN conjugates could inhibit the growth of tumors under laser irradiation. The inhibition of tumors was significantly better than that of the control group. GQDs/hMSN(DOX)-PEG exhibited a good photodynamic/chemotherapeutic effect on tumors at both cellular level and animal level. This work provided a basis for the application of GQDs hybrid nanoplatform in the treatment of tumors.

This work was under supervision by Dongzhi Yang. Jingjing Dong performed sample preparation and data analysis. Yan Zhang performed calculations. Pengyue Guo and Huanghuang Xu performed the cell level experiments. Jingjing Dong and Yuan Wang did the in vivo analysis. All authors read and contributed to the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (BK20161173), and Xuzhou Natural Science Foundation, China (KC18201, KC18108 and KC19066).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jddst.2020.102127.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Energy-free, singlet oxygen-based chemodynamic therapy for selective tumor treatment without dark toxicity
